notes: The XML identifies itself as CME00006 in IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID.
The source submodule is a distinct repository; this lineage record does not modify that artifact.

rights.basis: No file-specific license or rights statement was found in the artifact itself during this review.
rights.component: The repository XML artifact as a distinct digital file
notes: The age of the underlying texts and editions does not by itself establish the licensing of this particular XML artifact.

access.accessMethod: Open the U-M item page or stable handle
access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00006

entities.description: Public Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of selected portions of the 1827 edition.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00006
notes: The encoding includes the title page, printed pp. 1-147, and printed pp. 216-253, but not the complete 1827 volume.
No item-level statement establishing the original keying/OCR method or the rationale for this selection was found.

rights.basis: The item page states that the materials are in the public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement.
rights.component: The CME00006 digital text and materials presented by University of Michigan
notes: No Copyright-United States is not an affirmative worldwide license.
This statement must not be generalized to newly supplied British Library manuscript photography.

bibliographic.citation: Nicholas Harris Nicolas and Edward Tyrrell, eds., A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483 (London: Longman, Rees, Orme, Brown, and Green, 1827).
bibliographic.publisher: Longman, Rees, Orme, Brown, and Green
bibliographic.title: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483

entities.description: The printed compilation immediately underlying CME00006, combining a London chronicle, editorial notes and variants, documentary transcripts, poems, and later historical ballads.
entities.label: Nicolas and Tyrrell, A Chronicle of London (1827)
notes: The printed title's starting date 1089 is an error; the chronicle begins with 1189.
The complete book contains numbered pp. 1-274. CME00006 omits the dedication and preface, pp. 149-215, and pp. 254-274.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1827; U-M applies No Copyright-United States to its copy-derived materials, and Project Gutenberg distributes the book as public domain in the United States.
rights.component: The text and typography of the 1827 printed edition
notes: This does not decide rights in a newly created manuscript photograph or provider-imposed reproduction terms.

rights.basis: The imaging-service terms require written permission before supplied files are copied, reproduced, published, or otherwise reused; any permission and fees are separate from delivery of reference files.
rights.component: Any manuscript image files newly supplied by the British Library
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and any intended publication jurisdiction
notes: Request permission expressly before placing a supplied whole-codex bundle or any derivative image in the Git repository.

entities.description: A London-produced parchment codex written about 1444. It contains the principal early witness used by the 1827 editors, plus St Paul's tablet transcripts and the two long poems encoded in CME00006.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 565
notes: Work-bearing portions: St Paul's transcripts ff. 2r-9r; London Chronicle ff. 10r-99r; Henry V expedition poem ff. 102r-114r; Lydgate's Henry VI entry poem ff. 114v-124r.
The British Library record says digital images are currently unavailable. A legacy viewer object also failed during review.
A future facsimile request should cover the whole codex, not only these work-bearing folios.
The complete physical codex includes material not used in CME00006, including the king list on f. 1 and documents on ff. 100v-101v.

rights.basis: The imaging-service terms require written permission before supplied files are copied, reproduced, published, or otherwise reused; any permission and fees are separate from delivery of reference files.
rights.component: Any manuscript image files newly supplied by the British Library
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and any intended publication jurisdiction
notes: Request permission expressly before placing a supplied whole-codex bundle or any derivative image in the Git repository.

dateStatements.label: Chronicle manuscript
dateStatements.value: Last quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A London paper manuscript whose chronicle on ff. 2r-90v runs from 1189 to 1483. The 1827 editors used it for variants and for the continuation after Harley MS 565 ends.
entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Julius B I
notes: The exact sub-folio boundary at which the 1827 printed continuation begins has not been established.
No public page images were present in the current catalog record.
A future facsimile request should cover the whole codex and then point to the chronicle on ff. 2r-90v.
The chronicle occupies ff. 2r-90v; later material on ff. 91v-98v is early sixteenth century.

rights.basis: British Library imaging-service terms require separate written permission for reuse of supplied files.
rights.component: Any manuscript image files newly supplied by the British Library
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and any intended publication jurisdiction

entities.description: The composite Cotton manuscript containing the reconciliation poem printed on pp. 251-253 of the 1827 edition and encoded in CME00006.
entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Nero A VI
notes: A future facsimile request should cover the whole physical manuscript, then identify ff. 196v-197v as the CME00006-associated portion.
The relevant poem is on ff. 196v-197v within the cataloged component ff. 71-197.
The outer edges were damaged in the Cotton Library fire.

entities.description: A bound volume of Paston Letters and Papers containing John Bocking's letter to Sir John Fastolf on f. 41, ultimately excerpted in CME00006 through Fenn's 1787 edition and the 1827 Chronicle.

next_steps: Ask the British Library to check for existing complete color photography before commissioning new work.
For each order, request the complete physical codex, including covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every leaf where available, rather than only the work-bearing portion.
After receipt, keep the whole-codex bundle under source-cache and record image-level checksums or a bundle manifest.
Associate CME00006 with Harley MS 565 ff. 2r-99r and 102r-124r; Cotton MS Julius B I ff. 2r-90v; Cotton MS Nero A VI ff. 196v-197v; and Add MS 43488 f. 41.
Obtain written reuse permission before publishing any supplied images or bundles in the Git repository.
notes: Only two partial Harley images were found publicly: an 1827 plate of f. 37 and a modern crop of f. 68r.
The current BL rate card is per image and does not establish a final whole-codex price.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color facsimiles of all four physical manuscript codices be obtained and retained locally?

next_steps: Inspect a complete Cotton MS Julius B I facsimile when obtained.
Align the first post-Harley entry on printed p. 133 and the final 1483 entry against manuscript ff. 2r-90v.
Replace the current broad work-portion locator with the exact start and end folios.
openQuestions.question: Which exact folios of Cotton MS Julius B I correspond to the continuation printed on pp. 133-147 of the 1827 edition?

next_steps: Check U-M project documentation or contact Special Collections Research Center staff for production notes.
Compare the XML around PB REF233 and REF235 against the complete 1827 scan.
Record whether any unmarked text loss or only a milestone omission occurred.
openQuestions.question: What keying method and selection rationale produced CME00006, and why is the printed p. 234 milestone absent?

next_steps: Consult the manuscript's cited secondary bibliography and standard Middle English verse indexes.
Keep the current descriptive title and anonymous responsibility unless a reliable authority establishes more.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous Henry V expedition poem in Harley MS 565 ff. 102r-114r be assigned a more precise conventional title or attribution?

scope.notes: The circular is contextual legal guidance, not an item-specific rights determination or legal advice.

reviewNotes: This record models the 1827 book as a compilation, not as a single medieval work or a transcript of one codex.
Physical manuscripts, printed editions, digital encodings, and facsimiles remain separate entities even when the same passage connects them.
Local-cache coverage is stated relative to the entity represented: a complete printed-book scan may still be only partial evidence for a manuscript witness.
Whole-manuscript acquisition is the default for future facsimile requests; work-level associations are carried by folio locators rather than by trimming the acquired bundle.

lineage.summary: CME00006 is a selective digital encoding of Nicolas and Tyrrell's 1827 compilation. That edition prints an anonymous London chronicle from Harley MS 565, collates and continues it from Cotton MS Julius B I, and appends poems and documents from several witnesses and earlier printed sources. The complete 1827 book and several supporting editions are cached locally. No complete public facsimile was found for any of the four manuscript codices; the only public manuscript images found are an 1827 lithographic facsimile of Harley MS 565 f. 37 and a modern cropped detail of f. 68r. Future reproduction requests should ask for each complete physical codex, then associate the encoded works with the folio ranges recorded here.

catalogSummary.title: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483

catalogingSubject.label: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The 1827 edited compilation attributed in library catalogs to Nicholas Harris Nicolas and Edward Tyrrell: an anonymous civic chronicle whose actual narrative spans 1189-1483, editorial notes and manuscript variants, and appended contemporary letters and poems. CME00006 represents only the title page, printed pp. 1-147, and selected illustrative matter on pp. 216-253. This record keeps the compilation distinct from its component medieval works and documents, the 1827 printed edition, the U-M electronic selection, the repository XML, and every manuscript witness.

contentParts.label: Anonymous London Chronicle, 1189-1483
notes: DIV3 count is an encoding metric for annual containers and must not be treated as a canonical count of historical years or documents.
contentParts.summary: A predominantly prose civic chronicle arranged by reign and mayoral year. The 1827 text follows Harley MS 565 through its broken ending in 1443, records Cotton Julius B I variants, and prints the Cotton continuation through 1483.

contentParts.label: Manuscript variants, notes, and early documentary illustrations
notes: This entire printed range is omitted from CME00006.
contentParts.summary: Cotton Julius B I variants, Harley transcripts and translations of St Paul's tablets, and Latin, French, and English civic and royal documents preceding the long poems.

contentParts.label: Poem on Henry V's French expedition, Agincourt, and return to London
contentParts.summary: A three-passus Middle English historical poem preserved in Harley MS 565 and attributed to Lydgate by the 1827 editors.

contentParts.label: The Coming of King Henry VI from France to London
contentParts.summary: John Lydgate's Middle English account of Henry VI's triumphal entry into London after his French coronation, preserved in Harley MS 565.

contentParts.label: Materials on the 1458 reconciliation of Henry VI and the Yorkist lords
notes: The XML verse metrics count only the encoded Cotton Nero version.
contentParts.summary: An editorial note, an excerpt from John Bocking's letter to Sir John Fastolf, and two manuscript versions of the reconciliation poem. CME00006 includes the note, letter, and Cotton Nero A VI version but omits the Cotton Vespasian B XVI version.

contentParts.label: Later Lydgate and Lydgate-attributed ballads
notes: This concluding range is omitted from CME00006 and individual attributions should be checked against current poem-level scholarship before being expanded.
contentParts.summary: The concluding ballads, including material on civic dining, London Lickpenny, the empty purse, horned headdresses, and fraudulent millers and bakers.

dateStatements.display: The English London Chronicle tradition was first compiled in the early fifteenth century; the Harley version was brought through 1443
notes: This describes textual compilation, not the age of every event narrated or every later manuscript copy.

assertion.notes: This is a witness-production date and terminus for its version, not a single composition date for the whole chronicle.

dateStatements.display: Harley MS 565, written c. 1444 and carrying the chronicle through 1443

assertion.notes: The chronicle continues through events of 1483. Kingsford says this copy was made probably soon after Edward IV's death, while the British Library catalogs the relevant chronicle in the last quarter of the fifteenth century; neither statement supports a tighter normalized production range.

dateStatements.display: Continuation through Edward IV's death in 1483, preserved in Cotton MS Julius B I copied soon afterward

scope.description: The later continuation beyond the Harley ending

dateStatements.display: 15 March 1457/8 (15 March 1458 in modern New Style dating)
notes: The 1827 heading's 1457 follows historical English year reckoning; Fenn explains the Mid-Lent calculation, and the current BL catalog normalizes the date to 1458.

dateStatements.display: First published as the Nicolas-Tyrrell compilation in 1827

extent.description: The six sections are cataloging-level macro-parts of the 274-page printed compilation, not authorial divisions. The verse counts are XML representation metrics: 154 LG and 1,205 L elements in CME00006, including 67/592 in the Henry V expedition poem, 77/540 in the Henry VI entry poem, 8/64 in the reconciliation poem, and 2/9 embedded in the chronicle. The XML has 185 PB elements and omits substantial printed ranges.

formStatements.description: A prose civic chronicle and prose documentary/editorial matter accompanied by substantial Middle English and multilingual verse selections.
Predominantly prose annalistic narrative, with Latin headings and nine short verse lines represented in the XML.
Substantial civic, historical, and satirical poems selected as illustrations.

genres.label: Scholarly edition
notes: An edition-level genre; it does not describe the medieval chronicle or letters as nineteenth-century scholarship.

scope.description: Reign headings, marginal matter, manuscript variants, and documentary illustrations

languageStatements.label: Old French
notes: The 1827 preface calls the item French; this record uses the broad ISO 639-3 historical-language label rather than assigning a narrower dialect without further linguistic analysis.

scope.description: The 1296 French poem and related medieval documentary matter
Title, dedication, preface, translations, editorial introductions, and notes

notes: A complete 1827 edition scan and several supporting sources are cached locally; manuscript-image completeness and redistribution terms are handled only in the lineage manifest.
The exact preferred title is retained for bibliographic identity even though its opening year is demonstrably wrong.

next_steps: Consult the current DIMEV records keyed to Boffey numbers 969 and 3929 and recent Lydgate canon scholarship.
Retain component-specific attributed status rather than assigning Lydgate as author of the whole compilation.
openQuestions.question: What is the current scholarly attribution status of the Henry V expedition poem and the 1458 reconciliation poem beyond the 1827 editors' assignment to Lydgate?

next_steps: Consult a folio-keyed modern description or the manuscript when the complete images return.
Do not narrow the current ff. 2r-90v chronicle extent by inference from page count.
openQuestions.question: Which exact folios of Cotton MS Julius B I carry the 1443-1483 continuation printed on pp. 133-147?

next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any source delivery package from U-M.
Continue to describe CME00006 as a selected encoding, not a complete transcription of the 1827 volume.
openQuestions.question: What production history and selection policy caused CME00006 to omit the 1827 preliminaries, pp. 149-215, and pp. 254-274?

notes: This broad cultural origin does not force every selected document or manuscript witness into one exact place of composition.
The chronicle and editorial selection center London but also narrate events elsewhere in England, the British Isles, and France.
Civic organization by mayoral year and London subject matter support a strong association; this is not a claim that Harley MS 565 was physically copied in London.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous compilers and continuators of the London Chronicle
notes: The 1827 preface infers one original compiler and one continuator, while Kingsford's manuscript comparison demonstrates a more complex sequence of versions.

scope.description: The civic chronicle and its successive continuations through 1483

notes: Tyrrell signs the dedication; modern library records credit him jointly with Nicolas.

Selection, arrangement, transcription, and annotation of the appended illustrative materials

assertion.notes: The 1827 edition names Lydgate, but the current British Library component description calls this simply an English poem and does not repeat the attribution.

notes: The Harley colophon attributes the making to the monk of Bury.

assertion.notes: The 1827 editorial introduction invokes Lydgate, but the current British Library component record identifies the poem and folios without naming an author.

scope.description: The reconciliation poem, not Bocking's letter
Letter to Sir John Fastolf dated 15 March 1457/8

summaries.value: A composite antiquarian edition centered on an anonymous London civic chronicle arranged by reign and mayoral year. Its chronicle begins in 1189 despite the title's 1089, follows London officeholders and events through 1483, and is supplemented by manuscript variants, civic records, royal and private letters, and historical poems, many associated with John Lydgate.

The 274-page edition prints the main chronicle on pp. 1-147, Cotton manuscript variants and additional Harley texts on pp. 149-190, documentary and poetic illustrations on pp. 191-274, and a Harley fol. 37 facsimile plate in the preliminaries. CME00006 transcribes the title page, pp. 1-147, and only pp. 216-253 of the illustrations.

The 1827 volume's anonymous chronicle, Lydgate poems, Bocking letter, archival documents, editorial notes, printed facsimile, U-M selection, and manuscript witnesses are separate intellectual or material layers. Dates, authorship, folios, access, and image terms are asserted only at the layer supported by each source.

notes: The title's 1089 is a historical error retained as part of the established title; the chronicle begins with Richard I's coronation in 1189.
titles.value: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483

A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483; Written in the Fifteenth Century, and for the First Time Printed from MSS. in the British Museum: To Which Are Added Numerous Contemporary Illustrations, Consisting of Royal Letters, Poems, and Other Articles Descriptive of Public Events, or of the Manners and Customs of the Metropolis

notes: A discovery title that corrects the narrative's opening year without silently altering the 1827 publication title.
